claim: WHO's Global status report on road safety 2023 estimates 1.19 million annual road-traffic deaths and uses 2021 as the mortality-estimate baseline for its 2010–2021 progress assessment.

source.title: Global status report on road safety 2023

method: Reviewed the WHO publication record and report description for the annual death estimate, time coverage, mortality-estimate provenance, and country-profile scope.
limitations: The global estimate is not a city-level count and cannot identify a specific corridor or hospital burden.
The report combines country survey, legislation review, and modeled mortality estimates; it does not establish that a particular linkage method will reduce undercount in a given city.

claim: The World Bank's World Development Report 2021 states that official road-crash data captured only 56% of fatalities in low- and middle-income countries on average in its cited comparison.

source.title: World Development Report 2021: Repurposing data to make roads safer

method: Reviewed the World Bank spotlight for the stated capture percentage, its comparison basis, the description of data-system constraints, and the policy use case for geolocated crash records.
limitations: The 56% figure is an average from the cited comparison and is not a transferable correction factor for every city.
The source concerns fatality capture and does not by itself quantify non-fatal injury undercount or prove that police-hospital linkage is unbiased.

claim: WHO states that about 90% of injury-related deaths occur in low- and middle-income countries and identifies organized trauma care, transport to hospital, and prehospital and hospital care as relevant injury-system strategies.
method: Reviewed WHO's fact sheet sections on global distribution of injury deaths and organization of trauma care, including transport and prehospital components.
limitations: The fact sheet aggregates injuries and violence rather than isolating road traffic injuries in a specific city.
It describes relevant system strategies but does not estimate the effect of any particular allocation decision.

claim: The World Bank documents that Nairobi's paper police crash reports were digitized and geolocated because existing records were not being converted into useful and timely policy information for road-safety decisions.

source.title: World Development Report 2021: Repurposing data to make roads safer

method: Reviewed the Nairobi case description for the original record form, digitization and geolocation process, and stated policy rationale.
limitations: The Nairobi example demonstrates a data-use pathway, not a generalizable estimate of city-wide injury undercount.
The source does not establish that digitization alone changed road-safety outcomes or allocation decisions.
